flashcards.Back: A Cisco-proprietary Layer 2 protocol used to share information between directly connected Cisco devices, such as device IDs, capabilities, and software versions.
An industry-standard (802.1AB) discovery protocol that provides similar functionality to CDP but is vendor-neutral, allowing for discovery across different manufacturers.
The frequency at which a Cisco device sends CDP advertisements to its neighbors. The default value is 60 seconds.
The amount of time a device will retain information about a neighbor before discarding it if no new updates are received. The default is 180 seconds.
The standard frequency at which LLDP advertisements are transmitted. The default value on Cisco devices is 30 seconds.
The duration for which LLDP neighbor information remains valid in the local database. The default value on Cisco devices is 120 seconds.
The global configuration command used to enable CDP on an entire Cisco device. it is typically enabled by default.
The global configuration command used to enable LLDP on a Cisco device. Unlike CDP, LLDP is often disabled by default.
The interface-level command used to enable CDP on a specific port. this is necessary if CDP was globally enabled but specifically disabled on that port.
An interface-level command that allows a device to send LLDP advertisements out of a specific port.
An interface-level command that allows a device to process incoming LLDP advertisements on a specific port.
A verification command that provides a summary list of directly connected Cisco neighbors, including their ID, local port, and remote port.
A verification command that displays a summary of all discovered non-Cisco and Cisco neighbors using the LLDP protocol.
A command that displays exhaustive technical details for all discovered CDP neighbors, equivalent to using the 'detail' keyword.
The configured hostname of a neighboring device as discovered by CDP or LLDP.
A list of codes (e.g., R for Router, S for Switch) that identify the primary functions of a discovered neighbor device.
The specific physical port on the device you are currently logged into that connects to a discovered neighbor.
The physical interface on the remote neighbor device that is connected to your local port.
The structured data format used by LLDP to carry different pieces of information (like IP address or port description) within an advertisement.
The modern version of CDP that includes advanced features like native VLAN mismatch detection and duplex mismatch reporting.
A troubleshooting feature of CDP that alerts administrators if two connected switch ports are configured with different native VLANs.
A diagnostic function of CDP that identifies if two ends of a link have conflicting duplex settings (e.g., half vs full).
A command that displays the CDP status and timer settings for all active interfaces on a Cisco device.
A diagnostic command that displays statistics on the number of LLDP frames sent, received, and any errors encountered.
The amount of time an LLDP-enabled interface waits before attempting to re-establish discovery after a link flap or configuration change.
The management IP address of a neighboring device as reported in the 'detail' output of CDP or LLDP.
The specific hardware model of a discovered neighbor (e.g., Cisco 2960 or Catalyst 9300).
Media Endpoint Discovery. An extension of LLDP used specifically for identifying and configuring VoIP phones and other media devices.
The method of discovering neighbors using management frames that travel over the same physical cabling as user data traffic.
The process where a discovery entry is removed from the local table because its holdtime has expired without receiving a fresh update.
